metadata.title: Network Reconnaissance & Port Scanning
metadata.description: Master the art of discovery. Understand the mechanics behind Nmap, TCP handshakes, and how scanners map an infrastructure without being seen.

vulnerabilities.title: The TCP 3-Way Handshake
vulnerabilities.concept: Before any data is exchanged (like loading a webpage), the client and server must agree to talk. This is the 'Handshake'. Understanding this is critical because port scanners manipulate this process to probe servers.
technical_mechanics: Step 1 (SYN): The Client sends a packet with the SYN (Synchronize) flag. Message: 'I want to talk.'
Step 2 (SYN-ACK): The Server replies with SYN-ACK. Message: 'I hear you, and I am ready.'
Step 3 (ACK): The Client sends ACK. Message: 'Connection established.' Data transfer begins.
attack_vectors: TCP Connect Scan (-sT): The attacker performs the full 3-step handshake on every port. It is 100% accurate but very noisy because the server logs the completed connection.
SYN Flood: An attacker sends step 1 (SYN) millions of times but never does step 3 (ACK), crashing the server.
prevention_and_mitigation: Stateful Firewalls: Firewalls that track the state of the handshake and drop packets that arrive out of order.
Connection Logging: Monitoring logs for rapid connection creations from a single IP.

vulnerabilities.title: SYN 'Stealth' Scan (-sS)
vulnerabilities.concept: This is Nmap's default scan mode. It is called 'Stealth' or 'Half-Open' because the scanner never completes the connection, often bypassing application logs.
technical_mechanics: The Hook: The scanner sends a SYN packet (like a normal user).
The Response: The server replies with SYN-ACK (Port is Open) or RST (Port is Closed).
The Escape: Instead of sending the final ACK, the scanner sends a RST (Reset) to abruptly kill the conversation.
Result: The application layer never sees a connection, so it often doesn't log it.
attack_vectors: Mapping Attack Surface: Quickly finding open web servers (80), databases (3306), or SSH (22) without leaving a clear trace in application logs.
Bypassing Simple Filters: Some older firewalls only block established connections, missing these half-open probes.
prevention_and_mitigation: IDS/IPS: Intrusion Detection Systems (like Snort) look for the specific pattern of 'SYN then RST' to identify scanners.
Next-Gen Firewalls: Modern firewalls detect scanning behavior regardless of connection state.

vulnerabilities.title: UDP Scanning (-sU)
vulnerabilities.concept: UDP is a 'connectionless' protocol (used by DNS, DHCP, VPNs). It does not have a handshake, making it much harder and slower to scan.
technical_mechanics: Fire and Forget: The scanner sends an empty UDP packet to a port.
The Silence: If no reply comes back, the port is either 'Open' or 'Filtered' (Firewall dropped it). Nmap can't tell the difference easily.
The Error: If the server replies with an ICMP 'Port Unreachable' error, the port is definitely 'Closed'.
attack_vectors: DNS Amplification: Finding open DNS resolvers to use in DDoS attacks.
IoT Discovery: Many IoT devices communicate solely over UDP protocols.
prevention_and_mitigation: ICMP Rate Limiting: OSs limit how many 'Port Unreachable' errors they send per second (e.g., 1 per second). This makes UDP scanning extremely slow.
Deny All: Configure firewalls to drop all UDP traffic except for specific allowed services.

vulnerabilities.title: OS Fingerprinting (-O)
vulnerabilities.concept: Nmap can often guess exactly what operating system (Windows 10, Linux 2.6, iOS) a target is running by analyzing how its TCP/IP stack behaves.
technical_mechanics: The Query: Nmap sends malformed or weird packets (e.g., a packet with 'impossible' flags set).
The Quirk: Windows handles these weird packets slightly differently than Linux or MacOS.
The Match: Nmap compares the response quirks to a massive database of OS signatures to find a match.
attack_vectors: Targeted Exploits: If an attacker knows you are running 'Windows Server 2008', they will only attempt exploits that work on that version.
Network Inventory: Identifying unauthorized devices (like a rogue Raspberry Pi) on a corporate network.
prevention_and_mitigation: TCP/IP Hardening: modifying the OS network configuration to behave generically.
Firewall Scrubbing: Some firewalls rewrite packets to normalize them, hiding the quirks of the internal server.

vulnerabilities.title: Port States: Open, Closed, Filtered
vulnerabilities.concept: When reading a scan report, understanding the status is key. It tells you not just about the service, but about the firewall in between.
technical_mechanics: OPEN: An application is actively listening and accepting connections. (High Risk)
CLOSED: The packet reached the OS, but no application is listening on that port. (Low Risk)
FILTERED: The packet was dropped by a firewall. Nmap doesn't know if the port is open or closed. (Frustrating for attackers)
attack_vectors: Firewall Mapping: If ports 80 and 443 are 'Open' but port 22 is 'Filtered', the attacker knows a firewall is present and actively managing traffic.
Evasion: Attackers try to find 'Open' ports that shouldn't be (like a dev server on port 8080) to bypass the firewall.
prevention_and_mitigation: Rule of Least Privilege: Ensure only absolutely necessary ports are 'Open'.
Stealth Mode: Configure firewalls to silently drop packets (DROP) rather than rejecting them (REJECT), forcing the scanner to wait for a timeout.
